> "You are a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper writing a letter to your estranged daughter. You haven't spoken in five years. Tonight a terrible storm is coming and you're not sure you'll survive it. Write the letter."

**My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds you far from where you ought to be. Five years, Clara. Five long, lonely years since you left this house, this shore, and this father behind you. I’ve tried to forget the day you walked out, but the memory lingers like a ghost in the fog—cold, unyielding, and ever-present.

You were ten when I first noticed the change in you. You stopped playing with your dolls, stopped asking about the sea, stopped asking about me. And then, one day, you simply didn’t come home from school. I tried to find you, but you were gone. I suppose I should have followed.

I’ve spent those years alone, Clara, keeping the light burning for ships that never come. I’ve seen the sea in every direction, but I’ve never seen you. The lighthouse has become my prison, and the sea my only companion. I’ve grown old in this place, and I’ve learned to live with the silence.

But tonight—tonight is different. A storm is coming, one like none I’ve seen in my time. The wind howls like a beast, and the sea rages as if it would swallow the land whole. I’ve been keeping watch for hours, and I fear for my life. I’ve no doubt the waves will break through the walls, and the light will go out. But

**[Sealed with a wax emblem of a lighthouse and a storm]**  

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow, for five long years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and the weight of those years presses upon me like the storm that now roars upon the horizon. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the sea, that old and faithful companion, roars with a fury that would shake the very heavens. I fear I may not live to see the dawn, and yet I must speak, for I have waited too long to tell you the truth.

Eleanor, my dear, my lost child, I have not forgotten you. **To My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I write to you now with trembling hands, for the storm that approaches is the most formidable I have ever encountered. I fear it may not be the storm that will take my life, but the guilt of not having been there for you. 

Five years have passed since our last conversation, and I have not been able to bring myself to write to you. I have tried, many times, but each time the words would not come. Perhaps it is because I do not know what to say. I do not know if you still hold any affection for me, or if you have moved on. I only know that I have failed you, and I carry that failure like a stone upon my heart.

I have always believed that a father should be there for his child, and I have been absent for too long. I have been too wrapped up in my duties here at the lighthouse, and I have not given you the attention you deserved. I know that you must have felt abandoned, and I can only hope that this letter will serve as a means of making amends.

**April 2nd, 1893**  
**Lighthouse, North Sea Coast**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

If you are reading this, then I have either died in the storm or you have already come to visit me, which would be a miracle in itself. Either way, I suppose it is time we spoke.  

It has been five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and five years since I stopped counting the days. I have kept the lighthouse, as you would expect. I have kept the light burning, even when the sea howled and the sky turned black as pitch. I have kept the light burning, even when my hands shook and my bones ached.  

But tonight, something is different. The wind is not just howling—it is *screaming*. The waves are not just crashing—they are *roaring*. And I have never been more afraid in my life. Not of the sea, not of the dark, but of what this storm might do to me.  

I have not seen you in five years, Eleanor. Not once. Not even in the letters I have written, which you have never answered. I have written you every day, every night, every hour, if I had the strength. You have never replied.  

I have tried to believe you were busy. That you had a life. That you had a husband. That you had *children*. But I have also tried to believe

**April 3rd, 1889**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit here by the great brass lamp, the sea howling like a wounded beast outside, and I find myself writing to you. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though I have not forgotten you, I fear you have forgotten me.

I have always believed in the light, Eliza. Not just the light of the lighthouse, but the light of purpose, of duty, of love. And yet, I have spent these years in the solitude of this rock, with only the sea and the wind for company, and I have wondered if I was ever truly loved.

You were the light in my life, my little star. I remember the day you were born, the way your tiny hands gripped my finger as if to say, *I will not forget you.* But time, cruel and unrelenting, has taken its toll. I have watched you grow, and I have watched you leave. And I have not spoken, not truly spoken, for fear of what might come.

I know you must think me a cruel man, a distant father, a man who has let the sea take more from me than it should. But I have not let it. I have stood here, night after night, through the fiercest of storms, and I have kept the light burning, for you. For the memory of you. For the hope that

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, yet also with a flickering hope that perhaps, in this moment of peril, our paths may once more intertwine. The storm that now looms over these cliffs is not merely a tempest of wind and rain—it is a storm of my own soul, and I fear it may consume me before it consumes the sea.

You may read this letter in the days to come, or perhaps not at all, for I do not know if I shall survive the night. If you do, I beg you to take this letter and read it with care. Let it be a reminder of the love that once bound us, and of the sorrow that has kept us apart.

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I write to you now, as the storm gathers on the horizon, with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of years unspoken. **[Scrawled in shaky, uneven handwriting on the back of a weather report, the ink smudged by rain]**

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I hope you still remember who I am. I know you don’t. You’ve been gone for five years, and I’ve been trying to forget you too. But tonight... tonight the sea is restless, and the wind howls like it's trying to reach me. I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night.

The storm is coming. The waves are rising, crashing against the cliffs with a fury I’ve never seen. The lighthouse has stood for generations, but I feel it tremble beneath my feet. I’ve spent my life watching the sea, but tonight it feels like it’s watching me back.

I thought I was ready. I thought I had time. But the days keep passing, and I still haven’t written. I still haven’t said the words that have been lodged in my throat for so long.

I know you left. I know you don’t want to hear from me. But I can’t help it. I’ve been alone here, with only the light and the wind and the sound of the sea. And now, with the storm upon us, I feel the weight of it all—of the silence, of the guilt, of the love I never gave you.

I don’t know if you’ll ever forgive me.

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in sorrow, for I write with a heavy heart and a mind full of regret. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has worn away the years, it has not softened the pain of our silence. I write to you tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the wind howls like a mournful beast across the cliffs.

The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is dark with an ominous grey, as if the heavens themselves weep for the estrangement between us. I’ve spent these years watching the waves crash against the rocks, and each time I’ve heard their roar, I’ve thought of your voice, so full of life and laughter, and how it has been absent from my days.

I’ve often wondered if you still remember the stories I used to tell you by the fire, or if you’ve forgotten the lighthouse that once stood as a beacon of hope for those who sailed these waters. I’ve kept the light burning every night, just as I always did, and I’ve prayed that one day, you might come back to see it again.

I know I have not been the father you deserved, and I know I have not been the man you once loved. I have let my pride and my stubbornness cloud my judgment, and I have allowed the distance between us to grow wider than the sea. But I have not forgotten you,

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of silence that has grown louder than the storm. I have not spoken to you, Eliza. Not in letters, not in words, not in the hush of the night when the waves come in like ghosts. I have not spoken to you because I did not know what to say. And now, I fear, I may never have the chance.

I do not know if I will live through this. And if I do, I do not know if I will live through the silence that has followed me.

**A Letter to My Daughter**

My Dearest Eleanor,

As I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howls like a beast unleashed, rattling the windows and shaking the very foundations of this old stone tower. The sea, once calm and predictable, now roars with fury, and I feel the weight of the storm pressing down on me like the burden of years unspoken. Tonight, I am not certain I will see dawn. And yet, as I write these words, I find myself thinking not of the storm, but of you—my estranged daughter, my only child, and the one person I have failed to reach in five long years.

I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and the silence between us has grown as thick as the fog that often lingers over these cliffs. I have tried to forget, to move on, to let time heal the wounds that time cannot mend. But the memories remain—sharp, vivid, and unrelenting. I remember the way your eyes sparkled with the same determination that now burns in my own heart as I face this storm. I remember the laughter, the warmth, the love that once filled our home, now replaced by the coldness of distance and the ache of regret.

You were my light, Eleanor, and I have been the one who has kept it dim. I have been the one who has let the shadows of my own guilt and fear grow too large.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

If you're reading these words, it means I've survived another day, though I daresay I've not the strength for much more. The storm that brews on the horizon is a beast of a thing, with winds that howl like the very devil himself and waves that rise higher than the tallest of the cliffs. I fear for my life, and I fear for the light that guides the ships—though I shall not let it go out while I yet draw breath.

Five long years have passed since you last set foot in this tower, and I daresay you've not spoken a word to me since. I know you blame me for your mother's death, and I can scarce blame you. I was the one who failed to see the ship in the fog, and though I've spent these years in penance, I know you've not forgiven me. Yet, in this hour of need, I find myself yearning for your company, for your sharp tongue and your stubborn heart.
